ocar.opportunity: Comprehensive neural circuit mapping in the adult Drosophila brain required a whole-brain EM volume, but previous EM work in flies was limited to specific regions or larval brains, leaving the adult central brain inaccessible to synaptic-resolution connectomics.
ocar.challenge: Acquiring a complete EM volume of the adult Drosophila brain (~100,000 neurons) requires imaging tens of thousands of serial sections with nanometer precision, maintaining section quality over weeks of acquisition, and assembling the resulting terabytes of data without registration errors.
ocar.action: The authors prepared the adult Drosophila brain for serial-section transmission EM, collected a complete whole-brain dataset, and performed initial image alignment and quality assessment to produce a navigable 3D volume covering the entire central brain.
ocar.resolution: The study produced the first complete EM volume of the adult Drosophila brain, enabling systematic circuit reconstruction by the community. The dataset was made publicly available and has served as the foundation for multiple subsequent connectomics studies including the full central brain connectome.
ocar.future_work: The raw volume requires substantial reconstruction work; future efforts should develop automated segmentation pipelines capable of processing the entire dataset, and the imaging coverage should be extended to include the optic lobes and ventral nerve cord.

summaries.beginner: Scientists took thousands of ultra-thin slices of a fruit fly's adult brain and photographed each one with an electron microscope, then assembled all the photos into a complete three-dimensional map. This was the first time the entire adult fly brain was captured in such detail.
summaries.intermediate: This paper describes the acquisition and initial assembly of the first complete serial-section EM volume of the adult Drosophila melanogaster brain. The dataset provides a community resource for circuit reconstruction and has enabled multiple follow-up connectomics studies, including reconstructions of the mushroom body and central brain connectome.
summaries.advanced: The complete adult Drosophila EM volume described here represents a landmark infrastructure achievement, providing a whole-brain dataset at ~8 nm/pixel for circuit reconstruction. Technical challenges addressed include serial-section collection, alignment across thousands of sections, and handling of beam-induced distortions. The 2017 preprint preceded the formally published 2018 version (W2949808130); both describe essentially the same dataset. Community access to this volume has been foundational for subsequent dense reconstruction efforts. Coverage of optic lobes and VNC was incomplete in the initial release.

discussion_prompts: What strategies were used to handle section-to-section registration in a whole-brain serial-section EM dataset, and what are the main failure modes?
How does the scale of the adult Drosophila brain compare to other model organisms used in connectomics, and how does this affect the feasibility of dense whole-brain reconstruction?
What community infrastructure — data standards, repositories, proofreading platforms — was needed to make this dataset maximally useful for the neuroscience community?
